compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body: Brainstormed + decomposed (see session_log rich-keel-pen / 31d6). Focus: CODE drift only (problem-2 ticket-field invalidation already shipped in dead-stalk-chasm). Model: dependency-scope, NO attribution (sibling exemption dissolves; content-addressed per-file hash signed into the manifest). Children: Story 1 jerky-vista-amok (22bf) = drift detect+invalidate (full re-review) + new DET P9 empty-file-impact warning; fixes worm-folly-barge over-invalidation. Story 2 grace-thud-feint (5a6c, depends_on S1) = narrowed delta-re-review over the enforced CODEBASE_GROUNDED set + chained attestation; descoped read-set capture & >N-files fallback per brainstorm.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS 1 review — criterion T10 (Infrastructure / IaC) [overlay]: NOT APPLICABLE / PASS. No findings.

This plan concerns code-drift invalidation for plan-review attestations — application/review-pipeline logic: detecting material drift in the code a signed review was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files) and invalidating-or-flagging the attestation, cheaply, off the hot path. It provisions or configures no infrastructure: no cloud resources, no IaC (Terraform/CloudFormation/CDK/Pulumi/Ansible), and no Kubernetes/Helm. The "code it was grounded against" is source files resolved via the existing code-grounding oracle, not deployed infrastructure.

None of T10's binary checks engage: no remote-state/locking or plan-before-apply discipline, no IAM roles/policies or long-lived credentials, no idempotency/out-of-band-drift apply surface, no blast-radius / destroy-replace / prevent_destroy on stateful resources, no plaintext-secrets-in-IaC concern, no expensive/unbounded-resource sizing, and no new-infra logging/metrics/alarms or teardown. (Note: the AC's "drift" is CODE drift relative to a review, not Terraform/IaC configuration drift — so the T10(c) idempotency/drift check does not apply.) The signing/attestation machinery this builds on (parent epic 5fd2) is an HMAC-over-event-log scheme, not infra.

Per the T10 overlay gate ("apply only when the plan provisions or configures infrastructure ... else PASS not-applicable") and its ANTI-FP rule (not-applicable for non-infra tickets), T10 PASSES as not-applicable.

Affirmations: T10 — not-applicable (no infrastructure provisioning or configuration in scope).

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS 1 review — criterion T11 (Data-migration / backfill safety) [overlay]: PASS, not-applicable.

This plan ("Code-drift invalidation for plan-review attestations") adds a code-drift detection mechanism that invalidates or flags a plan-review attestation when the code it was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files) drifts after the review. It introduces no schema change to an existing persisted format and performs no data backfill:

- The drift signal is an ADDITIVE record over the parent epic's existing append-only attestation scheme (SIGNATURE head-state event + reducer-ignored REVIEW_RESULT sidecar). Going-forward attestations would carry a new grounding fingerprint; pre-feature attestations simply lack it and are treated as legacy/ungrounded — there is no in-place rewrite of existing rows and no backfill obligation stated or implied.
- No blocking DDL on a large table, no giant backfill transaction, no dual-write/cutover window, and no partial-failure data-loss surface exists for T11's binary checks (online/expand-contract, batching/throttling, resumability/idempotency, dual-write window, rollback/data-loss) to evaluate.

Per the T11 overlay gate ("apply only when the plan changes a schema/persisted format or backfills data; else PASS not-applicable") and its ANTI-FP rule (not-applicable for non-persisted/in-memory changes), this is a clean not-applicable. Consistent with the parent epic's own measured prior that rebar-domain "migration" tickets are routinely T11-not-applicable (recorded dry run: deterministic T11 fired 2/19 plans, all false — the bash→Python strangler, not a data migration).

Affirmations: [T11] not-applicable — no schema/persisted-format change and no data backfill in scope; additive-only record over an existing append-only event scheme.

No T11 findings.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 review — criterion T1 (prior-art / novel-architecture justification, overlay).

APPLICABILITY: a T1 bright-line fires. The epic proposes a NOVEL architectural pattern — content-/fingerprint-addressed freshness invalidation layered onto a cryptographic (HMAC) attestation — and carries a performance target ("cheap enough not to tax hot paths"). So the overlay applies and the plan should consider prior art before committing.

AFFIRMATIONS (T1 checks the epic TEXT satisfies):
- (c) No unverified capability assertions. The epic asserts no "library supports X" claim; the mechanism is described abstractly (detect material drift in the grounded files) and grounds itself by spinning out of a real, shipped predecessor (5fd2), whose attestation machinery exists in-repo (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py). Nothing unresolved to fail-closed on.
- Scoping is honest: the AC is decomposed into detect → invalidate/flag → tested, and explicitly bounds itself to "the code it was grounded against" rather than over-claiming whole-repo semantics.

FINDINGS:

- [ ] State, in the EPIC body, the prior art the drift mechanism is modeled on (content-addressed / Merkle input-hashing; Gerrit copyCondition `has:unchanged-files`; Bazel input-hashing; proof-caching dependency checksums) AND the existing in-repo binding it refines, so the novel-pattern choice is justified at the epic altitude rather than only in a child.
  - criteria: T1
  - location: epic description "Acceptance Criteria" line 1 ("A mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against (e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files).") + "Context"
  - evidence:
    - The epic text considers NO prior art and leaves the mechanism as a bare "e.g." — it does not name content-addressing, Merkle hashing, Bazel/Gerrit incremental-staleness precedents, or any established approach, despite proposing exactly that class of mechanism. ABSENCE rationale: for an overlay-triggering novel pattern, T1(a)/(b) want the prior-art consideration visible in the plan being committed to.
    - The relevant prior art DOES exist and the team HAS done the analysis — but it lives in the child story `22bf` ("This matches the consensus of every sound incremental-staleness system (Gerrit copyCondition has:unchanged-files, Bazel input-hashing, proof-caching dependency checksums)"), not in this epic. The epic-level plan a reviewer/implementer reads first therefore reads as reinventing a staleness check with no prior-art grounding.
    - Load-bearing IN-REPO prior art the epic omits: the attestation ALREADY has a freshness binding — `attest.py::claim_gate_check` invalidates on `result.head_sha != head` (coarse, whole-HEAD). The epic should frame its mechanism as REFINING this existing `head_sha` binding (whole-commit → grounded-file-scoped), not as a net-new capability; without that framing the relationship to the shipped mechanism (and the over-invalidation it causes) is invisible at epic level.
  - suggested_fix: Add a short "Prior art / relationship to existing binding" note to the epic: (1) name the existing coarse `head_sha` staleness binding in `attest.py` this refines; (2) name the established content-addressed/input-hashing precedents (Bazel input-hashing, Gerrit `copyCondition has:unchanged-files`, Merkle dependency checksums) the design adopts; (3) one line on why content-addressing (ancestry/rebase-independent) is chosen over the whole-commit binding. The child `22bf` already contains this material — lift the summary up to the epic.

This is the only T1 finding; the chunk is otherwise clean for this pass.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS 1 review — criterion T11 (Data-migration / backfill safety) [overlay]: PASS / not-applicable. No findings.

T11 is an overlay that applies ONLY when the plan changes a schema / persisted format or backfills data. This plan does neither as written: it specifies a MECHANISM to detect material code drift in the code a review was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files) and to invalidate or flag the attestation as stale when that code drifts. The three Acceptance Criteria are all mechanism/behaviour + test, not a persisted-format change or a data backfill:
- AC1 "A mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against (e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files)" — detection logic, no schema migration.
- AC2 "Drift invalidates or flags the attestation, prompting re-review; cheap enough not to tax hot paths" — invalidation/flag behaviour, no backfill.
- AC3 "Tested: code drift after a signed review -> attestation no longer valid" — a test, no migration-execution surface.

None of T11's binary checks engage: there is no online/expand-contract DDL, no large batched/throttled backfill, no resumable partial-migration, no dual-write window, and no partial-failure data-loss/rollback surface to evaluate. Per the overlay's ANTI-FP rule (not-applicable for non-persisted/in-memory changes), T11 PASSES as not-applicable.

Affirmations: [T11] not-applicable — the plan changes no schema/persisted format and backfills no data; it adds drift-detection + invalidation behaviour over the existing attestation, so the migration-EXECUTION safety overlay does not apply.

Disambiguation note (NOT a T11 finding): IF the implementation chooses to persist NEW grounding metadata on the attestation (e.g. the cited-file set or a code fingerprint) and existing signed attestations would need that field, the additive/coexistence concern for that new persisted data is the province of T12 (rollout/rollback) and the sibling parent epic's preserved-and-ignored event-rollout convention — not T11 migration-execution. The plan text does not affirmatively state such a persisted-format change, so it remains not-applicable here.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 PLAN REVIEW — [G6] Approach soundness, anti-patterns & alternative-selection. One MINOR finding (missing positive rationale in the epic body) + affirmations. Grounded the mechanism reasoning in the live attestation code.

AFFIRMATIONS (G6 sub-checks this plan satisfies):

- MECHANISM CORRECTNESS: The chosen mechanism — content-addressed (Merkle) per-file binding of the review's dependency set, NO per-commit attribution — actually achieves the goal and is sound against the edge cases G6 probes. Verified against source: today's freshness is a whole-HEAD exact match (`attest.py` `claim_gate_check` step 3, lines 96-102: `result.get("head_sha") != head` → `stale-head`) and `head_sha` is only audit metadata, NOT in the signed payload (`build_manifest` lines 33-41 omit it) — so the plan's premise (too-coarse in both directions; latent integrity gap) is accurate. The hard edge cases are handled: empty dependency set → conservative invalidate-on-any-commit fallback (child 22bf AC3); deletion / unexpected-creation → drift (22bf AC2); content-addressing makes it ancestry-independent (rebase/shared-checkout HEAD-move immune). No hidden ordering/atomicity/TOCTOU assumption at the epic level.

- FITNESS-FOR-PURPOSE: Solves the NAMED problem (code drift silently staling the attestation), not a proxy — it scopes freshness to "what the review depended on" rather than re-using the coarse whole-commit signal. The narrowed delta-re-review (child 5a6c) correctly narrows along the harness-ENFORCED `CODEBASE_GROUNDED = {E4,G1G2,A1,G6}` partition (verified the enforcement is a real, cited invariant), not along fragile per-commit attribution.

- APPROACH SELECTION (reviewer-generated alternatives): I generated two structurally-different alternatives and judged the chosen approach defensible. (a) Per-commit attribution / sibling-exemption (control-flow alternative) — the plan explicitly dissolves this (content-addressed hashing makes a sibling's edit to a shared file identical to any edit); defensibly simpler + more robust. (b) Observed read-set capture (data-source alternative) — explicitly considered and rejected in child 5a6c with a sound reason (a read does not imply load-bearing; casts too wide a net). Both are defensibly NOT superior → discarded, not written into the children (correct anti-priming). No clearly-superior missed alternative.

FINDING (G6.4 — positive rationale absent from the EPIC body):

- [ ] State the POSITIVE rationale for the chosen content-addressed-dependency-scope approach in the epic's own description, not only in the decomposition comment. The epic body's `## Context` states the PROBLEM (code can drift after review) and the success/acceptance criteria, but gives no "why content-addressed dependency-set scoping fits" rationale — that reasoning (dependency-scope, NO attribution, matches Gerrit `has:unchanged-files` / Bazel input-hashing / proof-caching checksums) lives ONLY in the post-decomposition comment and the children (22bf `## Why`). G6 requires the plan to state a positive rationale for the chosen approach; an epic whose description names the goal but not the chosen mechanism's fit leaves the next reader to reconstruct it from a comment.
  - criteria: G6
  - location: epic `## Context` / `## Success Criteria` (description body) — no rationale for the chosen approach; cf. the decomposition comment ("Model: dependency-scope, NO attribution; content-addressed per-file hash signed into the manifest") and child 22bf `## Why`.
  - evidence: the description body states only "the underlying CODE the plan reasons about can drift AFTER the review ... silently staling the attestation"; the chosen-approach rationale appears only in the 2026 decomposition comment and the children, not in the epic plan text reviewers/implementers read first.
  - suggested_fix: Add a one/two-line "Approach" note to the description, e.g. "Bind freshness to the CONTENT of the review's dependency files (file_impact ∪ cited files) via a signed content-hash map, NOT to the whole commit — matching the consensus of sound incremental-staleness systems (Gerrit has:unchanged-files, Bazel input-hashing); no per-commit attribution, so the sibling-exemption concern dissolves." This states the positive fit without listing rejected alternatives in the implementer-facing body.

Net: the approach is sound, fit-for-purpose, and defensibly best-available; the only G6 gap is that the epic's own body under-states the positive rationale that its comment + children already carry well.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 review — criterion T3 (Integration feasibility) [overlay]: PASS / not-applicable. No findings.

T3 applies only when a plan integrates an EXTERNAL API/CLI/service/library or asserts a capability it has not used before; otherwise it is not-applicable. This plan integrates none. Every mechanism it depends on is an INTERNAL, already-used rebar subsystem that the parent epic 5fd2 already consumes:

- Code-drift detection over "the resolved edit-set / cited files" rides on the shipped, polyglot code-grounding oracle (src/rebar/grounding/ — refute_absence/applies/scan/resolve, exported from grounding/__init__.py), the same facade Layer-1 already calls. The brainstorm's "content-addressed per-file hash signed into the manifest" is a stdlib file-hash, not an external dependency.
- Invalidate/flag-the-attestation reuses the existing HMAC-SHA256 signing + SIGNATURE-event + manifest scheme (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py, src/rebar/signing.py) — the manifest already binds a `material:` fingerprint and a git `head_sha`, so adding a grounding/code fingerprint is an additive line on a proven manifest, not a new integration. No new key custody.

Per the overlay's ANTI-FP rule ("an internal, already-used integration is not-applicable"), T3 PASSES. No external subcommand/endpoint to verify, no auth/HTTPS precondition, no asserted-but-unverified external capability, and no capability gap that would route to a SPIKE.

Affirmations (T3): integration-as-described is achievable via existing internals (no capability gap); named mechanisms (grounding oracle facade; the signing/manifest scheme) resolve to real, already-consumed in-repo source — MATCH; auth/HTTPS not applicable (no external service); no SPIKE needed.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 review — criterion T8 (LLM/prompt structural-completeness probe) [overlay]: PASS — no findings.

Applicability: T8 is an overlay that fires only when the plan DEFINES an LLM/agent system (prompts, sub-agents, reviewers, output schemas, enums). This plan does not: it is a deterministic drift-detection mechanism that compares the code an attestation was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files) to the current code state and invalidates-or-flags the attestation. It authors no prompt, sub-agent, reviewer, output schema, or enum vocabulary of its own. It is a CONSUMER of the attestation/coverage record produced out-of-band by the parent plan-review gate (5fd2). So the overlay's structural-gap probes are largely not-applicable; the few enum-like terms it references resolve to already-specified vocabulary elsewhere.

Affirmations (T8 checklist items this chunk satisfies):
- No schema/enum referenced whose value vocabulary is never defined. The drift mechanism reasons over the attestation's existing artifacts — the coverage record / cited files / resolved edit-set + the signature's head_sha binding — all of which are already-defined contracts from the parent gate (the SIGNATURE event's head_sha freshness binding + recorded coverage). The signature-verdict vocabulary it would extend (certified/mismatch/foreign_key/unsigned, plus the head-sha staleness signal) is fully enumerated in src/rebar/signing.py / verify_record, so the "stale/drifted" disposition maps onto an existing, defined vocabulary rather than an undefined hole.
- A processing protocol/decision rule is co-located with the schema that needs it. The decision rule (what counts as MATERIAL code drift → invalidate-or-flag) is co-located with the artifact it interprets (the grounding/coverage record the attestation already carries). Nothing is referenced-but-relocated within this plan's scope.
- No counter/state increment with ambiguous placement. The plan introduces no counter or state increment; it is a comparison + a single invalidate/flag transition.
- A fallback for an incomplete/failed sub-step is specified (to the extent the overlay applies). The Success Criterion phrases the outcome as "invalidated (or flagged stale)", i.e. an explicit, bounded disposition; and AC2's "cheap enough not to tax hot paths" constrains the mechanism's placement off the hot path. (This is a genuine design latitude — invalidate vs flag — not an undefined LLM enum; the parent gate already specifies fail-open/INDETERMINATE conventions the drift check can inherit.)
- No instruction-locality / pink-elephant antipattern; referenced agents/skills/enums exist. There is no LLM instruction surface in this plan; the referenced artifacts (the plan-review attestation, its coverage/cited-files record, the material-event-commit binding) all exist in the parent gate's shipped design (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/, src/rebar/signing.py).

No T8 findings to action. (Note: this PASS is scoped to the T8 overlay only; ordinary plan-completeness concerns about AC2's invalidate-vs-flag choice and the definition of "material" drift are outside T8's structural-gap remit and are left to the criteria that own them.)

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 review — criterion T3 (Integration feasibility) [overlay]: PASS / not-applicable, no findings.

T3 is an overlay that applies only when a plan integrates an external API/CLI/service/library or asserts a capability it has not used before. This plan does neither: it proposes an INTERNAL drift-detection mechanism over the code an existing plan-review attestation was grounded against (the resolved edit-set / cited files), invalidating-or-flagging the attestation on material code drift. The acceptance criteria name no external subcommand/endpoint, no new dependency, and no new auth/HTTPS surface — the work reuses rebar's own attestation/grounding/event machinery (the same internals the parent epic 5fd2 already binds the cert against). Per the T3 ANTI-FP rule ("an internal, already-used integration is not-applicable"), the overlay does not engage.

Affirmations (T3): not-applicable — no external integration; integration-as-described is achievable via existing internals; no capability gap is asserted; no SPIKE is required; no auth/HTTPS precondition applies (no external service).

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 review — criterion T1 (prior-art / novel-architecture justification, overlay).

APPLICABILITY: a T1 bright-line fires. The epic proposes a NOVEL architectural pattern — content-/fingerprint-addressed freshness invalidation layered onto a cryptographic (HMAC) attestation — and carries a performance target ("cheap enough not to tax hot paths"). The overlay therefore applies and the plan should make its prior-art consideration visible before committing.

AFFIRMATIONS (T1 checks the epic TEXT already satisfies):
- (c) No unverified capability assertions. The epic asserts no "library supports X" claim; it grounds itself in a real shipped predecessor (5fd2), whose attestation machinery exists in-repo (src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py — verified: `build_manifest`, `sign_plan_review`, `claim_gate_check`). Nothing unresolved to fail-closed on.
- Scoping is honest: the AC decomposes into detect → invalidate/flag → tested and explicitly bounds itself to "the code it was grounded against," not whole-repo semantics.

FINDING (T1(a)/(b) — prior art / novel-pattern justification not visible at the epic altitude):

- [ ] Surface, in the EPIC body, (1) the established prior art the drift mechanism adopts and (2) the existing in-repo binding it refines, so the novel-pattern choice is justified in the plan a reviewer/implementer reads first — not only in a child story.
  - criteria: T1
  - location: epic description "## Acceptance Criteria" line 1 ("A mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against (e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files).") + "## Context"
  - evidence:
    - The epic text considers NO prior art and leaves the mechanism as a bare "e.g." — it names no content-addressing / Merkle input-hashing / Bazel / Gerrit precedent, despite proposing exactly that class of mechanism. ABSENCE rationale: for an overlay-triggering novel pattern, T1(a)/(b) want the prior-art consideration present in the plan being committed to, at epic altitude.
    - The relevant prior art DOES exist and the team HAS done the analysis — but it lives in child story `22bf` ("This matches the consensus of every sound incremental-staleness system (Gerrit copyCondition has:unchanged-files, Bazel input-hashing, proof-caching dependency checksums)"), not in this epic. The epic-level plan therefore reads as reinventing a staleness check with no prior-art grounding.
    - Load-bearing IN-REPO prior art the epic omits (verified against source): the attestation ALREADY has a freshness binding — `attest.py::claim_gate_check` (lines 95-102) invalidates whenever `result.get("head_sha") != head` → `stale-head` (coarse, whole-HEAD), and `head_sha` is recorded as audit metadata but is NOT in the signed manifest (`build_manifest`, lines 33-41). The epic should frame its mechanism as REFINING this existing `head_sha` binding (whole-commit → grounded-file-scoped) and closing that latent signed-payload gap, not as a net-new capability; without that framing, the relationship to the shipped mechanism (and the over-invalidation it causes) is invisible at epic level.
  - suggested_fix: Add a short "Prior art / relationship to existing binding" note to the epic description: (1) name the existing coarse whole-HEAD `head_sha` staleness binding in `attest.py` this refines; (2) name the established content-addressed/input-hashing precedents (Bazel input-hashing, Gerrit `copyCondition has:unchanged-files`, Merkle dependency checksums) the design adopts; (3) one line on why content-addressing (ancestry/rebase-independent) is chosen over the whole-commit binding. Child `22bf`'s `## Why` already contains this material — lift the summary up to the epic body.

This is the only T1 finding; the chunk is otherwise clean for this pass.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 review — criterion T8 (LLM/prompt structural-completeness probe) [overlay]: PASS / not-applicable, no findings.

T8 is an overlay that applies only when the plan DEFINES an LLM/agent system (prompts, sub-agents, reviewers, output schemas, enums). This plan (code-drift invalidation for plan-review attestations) defines no such system — its scope is a deterministic drift-detection mechanism over the resolved edit-set / cited files that invalidates or flags an attestation when the grounded-against code changes. There is no new prompt, sub-agent, reviewer, output schema, enum vocabulary, processing protocol, counter, or sub-step needing a fallback introduced in the plan text. So the overlay's structural-gap probes do not engage.

Affirmations (T8 checklist — vacuously satisfied for this non-LLM plan):
- No schema/enum referenced whose value vocabulary is never defined: the plan references no enum/schema (it speaks of "material drift", "invalidated/flagged stale", the "resolved edit-set / cited files" — all mechanism-level, not enum vocabularies an executor must enumerate).
- No processing protocol/decision rule separated from a schema that needs it: no LLM decision protocol is defined here.
- No counter/state increment with ambiguous placement: none introduced.
- No unspecified fallback for a failed sub-step: there is no LLM/agent sub-step in scope.
- No instruction-locality / pink-elephant antipattern; no referenced agent/skill/enum that must be (and isn't) fully specified.

No T8 findings to action.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 review (G6 — approach soundness, anti-patterns & alternative-selection). Grounded the plan's premise + proposed mechanism against the shipped attestation code. Findings are coaching, non-blocking.

FINDINGS:

- [ ] Re-ground the plan's central premise — "the underlying CODE the plan reasons about can drift AFTER the review without any ticket edit, silently staling the attestation" — against the shipped attestation binding, because as written it appears contradicted by the existing `head_sha` git-state binding, which already invalidates the attestation on ANY code-HEAD movement (not just ticket edits). State whether the goal is genuinely a NEW capability or a MORE-PRECISE refinement of the existing coarse binding (FITNESS-FOR-PURPOSE / mechanism-correctness).
  - criteria: G6
  - location: plan "Context" ("the underlying CODE ... can drift AFTER the review without any ticket edit, silently staling the attestation") + Success Criteria ("invalidated ... when the code it was grounded against drifts materially, not only when the ticket is edited")
  - evidence:
    - `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:96-102` — the claim gate recomputes `signing.head_sha(repo_root)` and returns `verdict: "stale-head"` whenever `result.get("head_sha") != head`. So a code commit with no ticket edit ALREADY invalidates the attestation; the "silent staling" failure mode the Context describes does not match the shipped binding.
    - `docs/reuse-surface.md:91-94` — "A gate enforces freshness by recomputing the current HEAD and rejecting when `result['head_sha'] != head_sha` ... This binds an attestation to the code at review time."
    - Parent epic `5fd2` ties the signature to the latest MATERIAL TICKET-event commit for the TICKET-edit dimension; the CODE dimension is the separate `head_sha` binding above — both already fire on drift.
  - suggested_fix: Reframe the Context/Success-Criteria to acknowledge that ANY-code-drift is already caught by `head_sha`, and scope this ticket to the genuine residual: the existing binding is COARSE (it stales on ANY commit, including commits that touch no file the review was grounded against — a high false-invalidation / re-review-churn rate). The real goal then is the INVERSE of the stated one: invalidate only on MATERIAL code drift (the cited files / resolved edit-set) so unrelated commits do NOT needlessly stale the attestation. If instead a real silent-staling gap exists, cite the code path that bypasses the `head_sha` check.

- [ ] State a POSITIVE rationale for the chosen drift-detection mechanism (the AC names "the resolved edit-set / cited files" as the drift surface) and reason through its mechanism-correctness — specifically how the cited-file set is captured at review time and re-checked at claim time, and what happens when that set is empty/unresolved. G6(4) requires a positive rationale; as written the AC names a mechanism ("e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files") without stating WHY that surface is the right drift signal or how it is recorded on the attestation.
  - criteria: G6
  - location: plan Acceptance Criteria line 1 ("A mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against (e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files)") + line 2 ("cheap enough not to tax hot paths")
  - evidence:
    - The attestation manifest is a flat list of strings (`docs/reuse-surface.md` signing surface; `is_plan_review_manifest`), and the recorded freshness signal today is a single repo-wide `head_sha` — there is no per-file or per-edit-set hash captured. The plan does not specify how the cited-file set would be persisted on the attestation, so an implementer cannot tell whether to extend the manifest, add a sidecar field, or store per-file content digests.
    - EDGE/EMPTY case unhandled: many plan-review findings are NON-citable absence findings (parent `5fd2`: "plan findings are often NON-citable"), and the resolved edit-set can be empty/abstained — the plan must say whether an empty cited-set means "never drift-stale" (fall back to head_sha) or "always re-review", or the mechanism silently no-ops on exactly the plans most prone to drift.
  - suggested_fix: Specify (a) what is recorded at review time (e.g. content digests of the resolved cited files / edit-set, written to the attestation manifest or the REVIEW_RESULT sidecar), (b) the claim-path re-check (recompute digests, compare — keep it a pure-local check to honor "cheap enough not to tax hot paths", mirroring `attest.py`'s no-LLM/no-network claim gate), and (c) the empty/unresolved-set fallback (recommend: fall back to the existing `head_sha` binding so coverage never regresses). State why file-level digests beat the repo-wide `head_sha` (the positive rationale: fewer false invalidations on unrelated commits) at the point the mechanism is defined.

- [ ] Reconcile the proposed per-file/edit-set drift check with the EXISTING `head_sha` binding so the two do not compose into a contradictory or redundant gate (APPROACH SELECTION). The plan introduces a second freshness signal without saying how it relates to `head_sha`; if both are enforced as AND, the coarse `head_sha` check still stales on every unrelated commit and the new precise check buys nothing — i.e. the named problem is not actually solved.
  - criteria: G6
  - location: plan Acceptance Criteria lines 1-2 ("Drift invalidates or flags the attestation, prompting re-review") vs `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:95-102` (the existing `head_sha` freshness gate)
  - evidence: `attest.py:97` rejects on ANY `head_sha` mismatch BEFORE any material check runs. A new "material code drift" check layered on top is dead code unless the coarse `head_sha` gate is RELAXED to "HEAD recorded for audit, but freshness enforced by the per-file digest set instead." The plan does not name this required change to the existing gate, which is the load-bearing design decision.
  - suggested_fix: State explicitly that adopting material-code-drift detection REQUIRES demoting the repo-wide `head_sha == head` hard check (at `attest.py:97`) to a per-cited-file digest comparison (retaining `head_sha` as recorded audit metadata and as the empty-set fallback). Generate this as the single chosen approach; do not carry the rejected "keep both as AND" option into the implementer's plan.

APPROACH-SELECTION NOTE (G6.3): I generated two structurally-different alternatives and judged the plan's "edit-set/cited-files digest" approach defensibly best, PROVIDED the head_sha reconciliation above is made: (A) keep the coarse repo-wide `head_sha` binding and simply DOCUMENT that any code commit re-triggers review — structurally simplest, zero new state, but it is the status quo and produces high re-review churn (the very thing this ticket exists to reduce), so it does not advance the named goal; (B) bind to a content digest of the whole worktree subtree under the cited directories — coarser than per-file, simpler to compute, but stales on unrelated edits within those dirs. The plan's per-cited-file/edit-set digest approach is the most precise and is defensible on blast-radius (claim path stays a pure-local digest compare) and testability; discarding (A)/(B) per the anti-priming rule.

AFFIRMATIONS (G6 criteria this plan satisfies):
- FITNESS-FOR-PURPOSE direction is right: targeting "the code the review was grounded against (resolved edit-set / cited files)" is the correct drift surface for a plan-review attestation — it solves the named problem (silent code drift) rather than a proxy, once reconciled with the existing binding.
- COST awareness is present and correct: AC2 explicitly constrains the mechanism to be "cheap enough not to tax hot paths," matching the shipped claim-gate posture (a pure-local, no-LLM/no-network check at `attest.py`).
- TESTABILITY is specified: AC3 names the exact mechanism-correctness scenario ("code drift after a signed review -> attestation no longer valid"), which is the right end-to-end proving check for this gate.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS 1 review — criterion T1 (prior-art / novel-architecture justification, overlay).

A T1 bright-line plausibly fires: the plan proposes a NOVEL mechanism (code-drift detection that invalidates/flags a cryptographic attestation), which is a small architectural pattern with an established body of prior art it does not reference. The plan is a short epic stub, so this is a PLAN-under-specification finding, not a codebase-fact gap.

FINDING (T1(a) — prior art not considered before committing):
- [ ] Before committing to a drift-detection mechanism, name and weigh the established prior art so the approach isn't reinvented from scratch — both (a) the project's OWN already-shipped material-fingerprint binding (the parent `5fd2` attestation binds to the latest material event commit + a head_sha staleness check; see `src/rebar/signing.py` head_sha binding and `5fd2`'s attestation design), which this epic explicitly extends, and (b) the well-trodden external patterns for "has the input this artifact depended on changed": content-addressed / dependency hashing of the resolved edit-set (Bazel/Nix/build-cache staleness), test-impact-analysis / affected-file selection, and cache-invalidation-by-input-digest. State which the plan adopts/repackages and why a new pattern (if any) is justified vs. simply hashing the cited-file/edit-set contents at review time and re-checking on claim.
  - criteria: T1
  - location: plan "Acceptance Criteria" lines 1–2 ("A mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against (e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files)"; "Drift invalidates or flags the attestation … cheap enough not to tax hot paths")
  - evidence: ABSENCE — the plan names the goal (detect material code drift → invalidate/flag) and even a cheapness constraint, but cites NO prior art: it does not reference the parent epic's existing head_sha/material-fingerprint binding it builds on, nor any external dependency-hashing / test-impact / cache-invalidation precedent. T1(a) requires prior art be considered before committing.
  - suggested_fix: Add a short "Prior art / approach" note: (1) cross-reference `5fd2`'s material-fingerprint + head_sha staleness binding as the baseline this extends (so this is framed as an extension, not a fresh invention); (2) name the candidate established technique for the new part — e.g. record a content digest of the resolved edit-set / cited files on the attestation at review time and re-compare on the fast claim path (mirrors build-cache input-digest invalidation; keeps it off hot paths per AC2); (3) if a more novel approach is chosen instead, justify it against that baseline (anti-repackaging / Rule-of-Three).

AFFIRMATIONS (T1):
- T1(c) unverified-capability assertions: the plan makes no "library supports X" / external-capability claims to resolve — it stays within rebar's own attestation model, so there is nothing unverified to ground here.

This is the only T1 finding; the chunk is otherwise clean for this pass.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-1 review (G6 — approach soundness, anti-patterns & alternative-selection). Grounded against the shipped attestation code. Two coaching findings + affirmations.

FINDING 1 (G6 — mechanism-correctness / fitness: premise contradicted by the shipped binding):
- [ ] Re-ground the epic's central premise ("the underlying CODE … can drift AFTER the review without any ticket edit, silently staling the attestation") against `attest.py::claim_gate_check`, which ALREADY invalidates on ANY code-HEAD movement — so the "silent staling" framing is inaccurate and the real residual is the INVERSE (over-invalidation).
  - location: epic "## Context" + "## Success Criteria"
  - evidence: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py:96-102` recomputes `signing.head_sha(...)` and returns `verdict: "stale-head"` whenever `result.get("head_sha") != head`. A code commit with NO ticket edit therefore already invalidates the attestation — code drift is NOT silent today. The genuine gap (correctly captured in child 22bf's `## Why`) is that this binding is too COARSE: it stales on ANY commit, including ones touching no file the review was grounded against. The epic body states the goal as the opposite of what the residual actually is.
  - suggested_fix: Reframe Context/Success-Criteria to acknowledge whole-HEAD `head_sha` already catches any-code-drift, and scope this epic to material-drift PRECISION (invalidate only on the dependency files / cited set; do NOT needlessly stale on unrelated commits), lifting child 22bf's framing up to the epic body.

FINDING 2 (G6.4 — positive rationale absent from the epic body):
- [ ] State the POSITIVE rationale for the chosen content-addressed-dependency-scope approach in the epic's own description, not only in the decomposition comment / child 22bf.
  - location: epic "## Context" / "## Success Criteria" (no chosen-approach rationale)
  - evidence: the rationale (dependency-scope content-hash map, NO per-commit attribution; matches Gerrit `has:unchanged-files` / Bazel input-hashing / proof-caching checksums; closes the latent gap that `head_sha` is audit-only and NOT in the signed manifest per `build_manifest` lines 33-41) lives only in the comment + child, not in the plan reviewers/implementers read first.
  - suggested_fix: Add a one/two-line "Approach" note binding freshness to the CONTENT of the review's dependency files via a signed content-hash map (not the whole commit), stating why content-addressing (rebase/HEAD-move-immune, fewer false invalidations) fits — without listing rejected options in the implementer-facing body.

APPROACH-SELECTION NOTE (G6.3): generated two structurally-different alternatives — (A) keep coarse whole-HEAD binding + document that any commit re-triggers review (status quo; high churn, doesn't advance the goal); (B) per-commit attribution / sibling-exemption (control-flow; explicitly dissolved by content-addressing). The plan's per-cited-file/edit-set digest approach is defensibly best (claim path stays a pure-local digest compare; rebase-immune); both alternatives discarded per anti-priming.

AFFIRMATIONS (G6): fitness DIRECTION is right (the resolved edit-set / cited files is the correct drift surface once reconciled with the existing binding); cost-awareness present (AC2 "cheap enough not to tax hot paths" matches the shipped no-LLM/no-network claim gate); testability specified (AC3 names the end-to-end mechanism-correctness scenario); mechanism is sound at the child altitude (22bf handles empty-set fallback, deletion/creation, content-addressing edge cases).

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-2 INDEPENDENT VERIFICATION (findings 0–11). Verdicts with citations; no fixes.

[0] material-drift definition deferred. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=medium, blast=module, likelihood=high, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes (epic body says only "e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files"; no decision on authoritative signal or what "material" means at byte/AST/hash granularity); no_existing_mitigation=no (child 22bf §What/§Scope DECIDES it: dependency set = file_impact ∪ REVIEW_RESULT citations, whole-file content-hash; "material" = per-file content-hash mismatch); severity_claim_justified=insufficient; cited_reference_accurate=na. Net: the EPIC body defers it, but the design-of-record (child) resolves it; a charitable read finds the choice made, not abandoned.

[1] invalidate-OR-flag undecided. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=low, blast=module, likelihood=medium, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes (epic SC + AC both say "invalidated (or flagged stale)" / "invalidates or flags"); no_existing_mitigation=no (child 22bf decides BLOCK/invalidate — "Any mismatch … = drift → block the claim"); cited_reference_accurate=na. The epic body is genuinely ambiguous; the child commits to invalidate.

[2] "cheap enough not to tax hot paths" unbounded. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=low, blast=local, likelihood=medium, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes at epic level; no_existing_mitigation=no (child 22bf: "sub-ms for a handful of files; no claim hot-path regression"; the claim path is the documented hot path — attest.py claim_gate_check is a pure-local check); cited_reference_accurate=na. Child names the hot path (claim) and a bound (sub-ms).

[3] ticket-event binding vs new code-grounding signal coexistence. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=low, blast=module, likelihood=medium, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes (epic body does not describe how the two bindings compose); no_existing_mitigation=no (the existing head_sha freshness gate IS the code binding and child 22bf states it replaces step-3 whole-HEAD match; material-fingerprint stays for ticket edits — attest.py:96-123). Real epic-body gap; resolved in child. cited_reference_accurate=na.

[4] rollout treatment of already-signed attestations / back-out. ATTRS: prod_impact=medium, debt=low, blast=system, likelihood=medium, reversibility=moderate. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes at epic level (epic body silent on retroactive vs grandfathered + false-invalidation back-out); no_existing_mitigation=insufficient (child 22bf has an empty-set conservative fallback and a36b/worm-folly-barge addresses false-invalidations, but neither states rollout treatment of pre-feature signed attestations explicitly; parent 5fd2's gate is opt-in/default-off, which softens blast but is not cited here). path_reachable=yes; cited_reference_accurate=na. A genuine residual at epic scope.

[5] no ADR/CLAUDE.md/design-doc artifact named. ATTRS: prod_impact=none, debt=medium, blast=module, likelihood=medium, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes (epic body names no doc artifact); no_existing_mitigation=insufficient (child 5a6c AC requires "Descope is documented in code/docs"; child 22bf updates det_floor docstring — partial doc commitments exist for sub-parts, but no architectural-decision doc for the drift-binding decision is named anywhere). cited_reference="AC1" is accurate as a paraphrase. cited_reference_accurate=na (no specific code line cited).

[6] configurability of the drift threshold/scope unstated. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=low, blast=module, likelihood=low, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes (neither epic nor children state whether the dependency-set scope / "material" rule is configurable vs hardcoded); no_existing_mitigation=yes (no commitment to configurability found); severity_claim_justified=insufficient (threshold is content-hash exact-match, not a tunable magnitude — "likely to evolve" is weaker here than for a numeric threshold). cited_reference_accurate=na.

[7] AC2 couples correctness + perf without sizing/sequencing. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=low, blast=module, likelihood=low, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=partial at epic level; no_existing_mitigation=no (child 22bf carries file_impact = 3 named files + an explicit perf AC "sub-ms … no claim hot-path regression"; child 5a6c carries a measurement AC; the epic IS decomposed into a risk-ordered Story1→Story2 sequence per the decomposition comment). The sizing/sequencing the finding asks for exists at child altitude. cited_reference_accurate=na.

[8] premise "code drift is silent" contradicted by shipped head_sha binding; real residual is over-invalidation. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=medium, blast=module, likelihood=high, reversibility=easy. cited_reference_accurate=YES — attest.py:96-102 recomputes signing.head_sha and returns verdict "stale-head" when result['head_sha'] != head, so a code commit with no ticket edit already invalidates (VERIFIED in source). is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes (epic Context frames it as "silently staling"; the shipped behavior is the inverse — over-invalidation); no_existing_mitigation=insufficient (child 22bf §Why AND child a36b/worm-folly-barge DO name the over-invalidation precisely, and the epic ALREADY carries G6 review comments stating exactly this reframing — but the epic Context/Success-Criteria BODY still reads as under-invalidation). impact_follows_necessarily=yes. Strong, code-grounded finding; the residual it names lives in children + review comments, not the implementer-facing epic body.

[9] no positive rationale for content-addressed dependency-scope in epic body. ATTRS: prod_impact=none, debt=low, blast=module, likelihood=high, reversibility=easy. cited_reference_accurate=YES — build_manifest (signing.py:33-41 analog / attest.py:33-41) omits head_sha from the signed manifest; signing.py:19-25 confirms the signed payload deliberately excludes head_sha (audit metadata only) — so the "latent integrity gap" claim is accurate. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes (epic body states only the problem + detect→invalidate/flag→tested; the content-addressed/Bazel/Gerrit rationale lives only in the 2026 decomposition comment + child 22bf §Why); no_existing_mitigation=insufficient (rationale exists, just not in the epic body — a G6 review comment on THIS epic already raised it). Accurate but largely a duplicate of a self-surfaced G6 finding already on the ticket.

[10] log child a36b requires ANY-commit-invalidates; plan narrows to material-only, "silently dropping" it. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=low, blast=module, likelihood=low, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=NO. CITATION MISREAD: child a36b/worm-folly-barge is a BUG titled "ANY new commit (even unrelated) invalidates a fresh attestation" describing the CURRENT over-invalidation as a DEFECT to FIX; its ACs require scoping freshness to the cited/edit-set files so an unrelated commit does NOT stale (the opposite of "require any-commit-invalidates"). The "any-commit-invalidates" is the empty-set CONSERVATIVE FALLBACK in child 22bf (only for unscopable tickets), not a broad dropped requirement. So the design-of-record is consistent with the plan, not narrowed against it. evidence_entails_finding=no; no_existing_mitigation=no. cited_reference_accurate=no (the child says the inverse of what the finding asserts it requires).

[11] log child 5a6c narrowed delta-re-review dropped from the plan. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=low, blast=module, likelihood=low, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=NO. Child 5a6c (grace-thud-feint) is a LIVE story under this epic (parent_id=4fcd, depends_on 22bf) explicitly carrying the narrowed delta-re-review; the decomposition comment states "Story 2 … = narrowed delta-re-review … (depends_on S1)." It is deliberately a SEPARATE, sequenced story, not silently dropped — the epic's 3 ACs are intentionally the minimal success contract, with the narrowed-re-review elaborated in its own child. no_existing_mitigation=no; evidence_entails_finding=no. cited_reference_accurate=na (the log child is correctly described, but the inference that it was dropped is wrong — it is an open child of this epic).

Method note: grounded against attest.py:96-124, signing.py:19-25/319-360, and tickets 5fd2/22bf/a36b/5a6c. Findings 8/9 cite accurate code; 10/11 rest on an inference contradicted by the live ticket graph.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body: PASS-2 (independent re-verification, findings 0–11). Code-grounded against attest.py:96–124 + build_manifest (33–41), and ticket graph (children 22bf/5a6c, sibling-bug a36b). Key results: [8] cited code accurate — head_sha mismatch already invalidates on any commit, so "silent staling" is the inverse of shipped behavior; epic BODY still frames it as under-invalidation though children/G6-comments name over-invalidation. [9] cited code accurate — build_manifest omits head_sha from the signed payload; content-addressed rationale lives only in the decomposition comment/22bf, not the epic body (largely duplicates a self-surfaced G6 finding). [10] citation MISREAD — a36b titles "ANY new commit invalidates" as a BUG TO FIX (over-invalidation), not a requirement the plan drops; the any-commit behavior is only the empty-set conservative FALLBACK in 22bf. [11] child 5a6c (narrowed delta-re-review) is a LIVE open story under this epic (parent 4fcd, depends_on 22bf), deliberately sequenced, not silently dropped. [0–3] genuine epic-body gaps but resolved in design-of-record children. [4] genuine epic-altitude residual (rollout of pre-signed attestations). [5/6/7] partial/weak.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:

Strong, code-grounded finding; residual lives in children + comments, not the implementer-facing epic body.

[9] no positive rationale for content-addressed dependency-scope in epic body. [10] (G3) child 5a6c does not cover parent AC1; correctly scoped to post-detection remedy. ATTRS: prod_impact=none, debt=none, blast=local, likelihood=low, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes — the finding's own assertion is that this is NOT a coverage gap (sibling 22bf fully owns AC1; 5a6c optimizes the remedy on top of Story-1 detection). VERIFIED: 22bf AC binds a signed {path: content-hash} map and removes the whole-HEAD match (owns AC1); 5a6c depends_on 22bf and re-runs only {P2,P3} ∪ CODEBASE_GROUNDED. The 4-bucket assignment (structural/orthogonal to AC1, no uncovered criterion) is correct; no_existing_mitigation=na; cited_reference_accurate=yes (both sibling ACs quoted accurately).

[11] (G4) scope overlap a36b ↔ 22bf over the same scoped-freshness behavior. ATTRS: prod_impact=low, debt=low, blast=module, likelihood=medium, reversibility=easy. is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=yes — VERIFIED: a36b AC1 scopes freshness to "resolved edit-set / cited files rather than the whole HEAD sha" over attest.py claim_gate_check; 22bf description says "fixes worm-folly-barge" and 22bf AC2 re-specifies the same behavior ("Claim SUCCEEDS when an unrelated file changed … claim is BLOCKED when a dependency file's content changed"). Both target src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py claim_gate_check whole-HEAD-match removal — genuine duplicate/overlapping ownership of one behavior across two siblings. no_existing_mitigation=insufficient — a G4 container-review comment ALREADY on a36b proposes making it a non-implementing bug that 22bf "fixes," so the overlap is flagged but not yet resolved in the ticket structure; cited_reference_accurate=yes (all four quotes match the live tickets).

Method note: findings 8 and 9 cite accurate, verified code (attest.py:96–102; build_manifest 33–41 + __init__.py:81). Findings 0–4 are genuine epic-body gaps largely resolved in the design-of-record children (0–3) or a genuine epic-scope residual (4). 10 and 11 are accurate structural/overlap observations consistent with the live ticket graph; 11's overlap is real and only partially mitigated by an existing review comment.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
PASS-2 independent verification (findings 12–13). Verdicts with citations; no fixes.

[12] CLAIM: child a36b expresses an ANY-new-commit-invalidates REQUIREMENT that the plan contradicts by narrowing to material-only drift. ATTRS: prod_impact=none, debt_impact=low, blast_radius=module, likelihood=low, reversibility=easy. SUB-ANSWERS: is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=NO; path_reachable=no; impact_follows_necessarily=no; no_viable_alternative_explanation=no; no_existing_mitigation=no; severity_claim_justified=no; cited_reference_accurate=no.
RATIONALE: a36b ("worm-folly-barge") is a BUG ticket whose title NAMES the any-commit-invalidates behavior as a DEFECT to FIX (over-invalidation friction), not a requirement to preserve. Its description: "ANY subsequent commit — even one in a completely unrelated file — invalidates a freshly-earned, still-correct attestation." Its Expected result: "The claim succeeds: the attestation is still valid because none of the code ticketA's plan was grounded against changed." Its AC1: "Claim freshness is scoped to the code the review was grounded against (resolved edit-set / cited files) rather than the whole HEAD sha, so an unrelated commit does NOT stale the attestation." This is the SAME material-only scoping the plan adopts — the plan AGREES with a36b, it does not narrow away from it. The "ANY new commit invalidates" behavior survives only as the empty-set CONSERVATIVE FALLBACK in sibling story 22bf (for unscopable tickets with no file_impact and no citations), not as a broad dropped requirement. The finding inverts the child's intent; the citation is misread.

[13] CLAIM: child 5a6c's narrowed delta-re-review decision is silently dropped from the plan ("only says drift prompts re-review"). ATTRS: prod_impact=none, debt_impact=low, blast_radius=module, likelihood=low, reversibility=easy. SUB-ANSWERS: is_verifiable=yes; evidence_entails_finding=NO; path_reachable=no; impact_follows_necessarily=no; no_viable_alternative_explanation=no; no_existing_mitigation=no; severity_claim_justified=no; cited_reference_accurate=na.
RATIONALE: 5a6c ("grace-thud-feint") is a LIVE OPEN STORY under THIS epic (parent_id=4fcd-449a-d1b3-4a23; depends_on 22bf), titled "Narrowed delta-re-review on code drift: re-run only the enforced code-grounded criteria with a chained attestation," carrying the full narrowed-re-review decision (re-run only {P2,P3} ∪ applicable CODEBASE_GROUNDED, reuse cached findings, chained attestation, fail-safe to full re-review on version skew). The epic's decomposition comment states: "Story 2 grace-thud-feint (5a6c, depends_on S1) = narrowed delta-re-review over the enforced CODEBASE_GROUNDED set + chained attestation." So the narrowed re-review is NOT dropped — it is a deliberately separated, dependency-sequenced child story. The epic's 3 ACs are intentionally the minimal success contract (detect → invalidate/flag → tested), with the narrowed-re-review scope elaborated in its own child by design. A plan that decomposes a refinement into a sequenced child story has not "silently dropped" it. cited_reference_accurate=na (no specific code line cited; the log child is correctly named but the inference that it was dropped is wrong).

Both findings share one root error: treating a child ticket's title/existence as an externally-expressed requirement the plan silently contradicts or omits, when the children ARE the plan's own decomposition that resolves these very points (22bf+5a6c are the two implementing stories; a36b is a sibling bug whose fix the plan adopts). This duplicates the conclusions of the prior PASS-2 verifications already on this ticket (their findings [10]/[11]).

[12] a36b ("worm-folly-barge") is a BUG whose title/description name "ANY new commit (even unrelated) invalidates a fresh attestation" as the DEFECT TO FIX, not a requirement the plan must honor. a36b AC1: "Claim freshness is scoped to the code the review was grounded against (resolved edit-set / cited files) rather than the whole HEAD sha, so an unrelated commit does NOT stale the attestation." compiled_state.description:
## Context

A plan-review attestation must stay valid exactly as long as the thing it certifies — the plan,
reviewed against the code it reasons about — is unchanged. Two facets of the current binding are
wrong, and this epic fixes both (decomposition + full rationale: session log `rich-keel-pen`,
ADR `docs/adr/0002-code-drift-invalidation.md`):

- **Over-invalidation (the LIVE bug — `worm-folly-barge`/a36b).** `attest.py::claim_gate_check`
  binds freshness to the exact code HEAD sha, so ANY commit — even an unrelated file or a docs
  typo — invalidates a freshly-earned, still-correct attestation, forcing a slow, billable
  re-review for no semantic reason. (So code drift is NOT "silent" today — the binding is simply
  too COARSE; the original framing of this epic as a pure under-invalidation problem was wrong and
  is corrected here.)
- **Under-invalidation (precision goal).** Because the binding is whole-HEAD rather than scoped to
  the code the review depended on, once we narrow it we must NOT lose the ability to detect that the
  relevant code actually changed.

Both share one root cause: the code-binding is whole-HEAD instead of scoped to the review's
dependency set. The material-fingerprint binding (problem-2, already shipped in `dead-stalk-chasm`:
invalidate on description/AC/file_impact/decomposition edits, not on tags/comments/links/assignee)
is correct and stays; this epic adds a SEPARATE, independent code-drift check ANDed with it.

## Chosen approach (and why)

**Content-addressed, dependency-scoped, no attribution.** Invalidate on *what the review depended
on*, not *who/what changed the rest of the tree*. This is the consensus of every sound
incremental-staleness system (Gerrit `copyCondition` `has:unchanged-files`, Bazel input-hashing,
proof-caching dependency checksums) — none decide staleness by attributing a change to an author, so
the "sibling exemption" concern dissolves: a sibling's edit to a shared file is treated like any
edit. Concretely:

- **Dependency set** = the ticket's declared `file_impact` ∪ the files cited in its `REVIEW_RESULT`
  findings (whole-file granularity in v1; region/AST narrowing is a possible future, not configured
  now).
- **Binding** = a per-path `{path: content-hash}` map stored INSIDE the signed manifest (so drift
  detection is itself tamper-evident; today `head_sha` is unsigned audit metadata). Content-
  addressing is ancestry-independent — immune to rebases and shared-checkout HEAD moves.
- **Decision = INVALIDATE (hard block), not flag-stale.** A drifted dependency blocks the claim and
  prompts re-review, consistent with the existing gate (absent/stale signature already blocks). We do
  NOT ship a soft warning for drift.
- **Empty dependency set** (no `file_impact`, no citations) → conservative fallback: invalidate on
  any commit (today's safe behavior, now only for unscopable tickets). A new DET-floor check (`P9`)
  emits an advisory nudge to populate `file_impact` so scoping works.
- **Re-review may be narrowed (Story 2).** On pure code drift, re-running only the enforced
  code-grounded criteria (`CODEBASE_GROUNDED`) is sound and cheaper than a full re-review; the
  full re-review is the fallback.

## Hot-path budget

The drift check runs on the **claim path** (the fast, no-LLM, no-network HMAC verify; target p95
< ~50ms). Hashing the handful of dependency files is sub-millisecond, so it stays well within that
budget; the check must add no LLM/network call to claim.

## Rollout / back-out

The check lives behind the existing `verify.require_plan_review_for_claim` gate. Attestations signed
*before* this lands carry no `{path: hash}` map in their manifest; on first claim under the new code
they are treated as needing re-review (re-run `review-plan` to earn a map-bearing attestation) —
acceptable because re-review is the normal recovery. Back-out = revert the drift check; the
material-fingerprint binding is untouched, so reverting cannot corrupt existing attestations.

## Decomposition

- **Story 1 — `jerky-vista-amok` (22bf):** drift detection + invalidation (full re-review on drift)
  + the `P9` empty-`file_impact` DET warning. Fixes `worm-folly-barge`.
- **Story 2 — `grace-thud-feint` (5a6c, depends_on Story 1):** narrowed delta-re-review over the
  enforced code-grounded set + chained attestation; measured before default-on.
- **Bug — `worm-folly-barge` (a36b):** the over-invalidation failing-case report; its remedy is
  owned and implemented by Story 1 and it closes when Story 1 lands.

## Success Criteria

- An attestation is invalidated when the code it was grounded against (its dependency set) drifts
  materially, AND is NOT invalidated by changes outside that set.
- The drift check is content-addressed and cheap enough to run on the claim hot path.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] A mechanism detects material drift in the review's dependency set (`file_impact` ∪ cited
      files), via a per-path content-hash map signed into the attestation manifest.
- [ ] Drift **invalidates** the attestation (hard block, prompting re-review); an unrelated change
      does NOT invalidate (fixes `worm-folly-barge`). Empty dependency set → conservative fallback.
- [ ] The check runs on the claim path within its latency budget (no LLM/network; sub-ms hashing).
- [ ] The novel decision is documented (ADR `0002-code-drift-invalidation.md`) and the manifest
      payload shape is pinned so Story 2's per-finding code-hash vintage composes on it.
- [ ] Tested: code drift in a dependency file after a signed review → attestation invalid; an
      unrelated commit → attestation stays valid.
compiled_state.title: Code-drift invalidation for plan-review attestations
